title: Acute Abdominal Pain
summary: Sudden or severe abdominal pain that needs careful assessment. Some causes are manageable at home. Others — appendicitis, ectopic pregnancy, obstruction — are surgical emergencies.
steps: Locate the pain precisely. Ask the person to point with one finger to where it hurts most. The location is the most important diagnostic clue.
RIGHT LOWER ABDOMEN — appendicitis territory. Pain that starts around the navel and moves to the right lower abdomen, worsens over hours, with fever and nausea. This is a surgical emergency.
UPPER ABDOMEN — gastritis, peptic ulcer, or pancreatitis. Burning or gnawing pain, worse after eating or with alcohol.
LOWER ABDOMEN IN WOMEN — ovarian cyst, ectopic pregnancy, pelvic inflammatory disease. One-sided pain in a woman of reproductive age needs urgent assessment.
GENERALISED ABDOMEN — peritonitis, bowel obstruction. Board-like rigid abdomen with severe diffuse pain is a surgical emergency.
Assess the severity: Can the person stand up straight? Does movement or pressing make it much worse? Is there fever? Is there vomiting without diarrhoea?
Position of comfort: most people with abdominal pain prefer to lie still. Encourage the least painful position — usually knees drawn up.
Do not give food or water if surgery may be needed.
Monitor closely — note whether pain is improving, staying the same, or worsening over 30 to 60 minutes.
warnings: Do not give strong painkillers that mask symptoms — this can delay diagnosis of a surgical emergency.
Do not apply heat to an acutely painful abdomen — can worsen inflammation.
Do not give laxatives for abdominal pain — dangerous if bowel obstruction is present.
A rigid board-like abdomen is always a surgical emergency.
redFlags: Pain in the right lower abdomen that is worsening over hours — possible appendicitis.
Rigid board-like abdomen — peritonitis or perforation.
One-sided pelvic pain with positive pregnancy test — possible ectopic pregnancy.
Severe pain with vomiting and no bowel movement for more than 24 hours — possible obstruction.
Vomiting blood or passing black tarry stool with abdominal pain.

helpful: Paracetamol for mild to moderate pain — does not mask surgical symptoms the way stronger drugs do
Antacids for confirmed gastric pain — aluminium hydroxide or omeprazole
avoid: Ibuprofen and aspirin worsen gastric ulcers and cause stomach bleeding
Laxatives with any abdominal pain until cause is known
askFor: Urgent medical assessment for any abdominal pain with fever
Pregnancy test for any woman of reproductive age with lower abdominal pain
Omeprazole for gastric pain with known ulcer history

whenToGetHelp: Go to hospital immediately for pain in the right lower abdomen that is worsening, a rigid abdomen, one-sided pelvic pain in a woman who could be pregnant, pain with vomiting and no bowel movements, or any severe pain with fever. These are potential surgical emergencies.
